serving.natural_language_portion: One plate of scorching ocean spaghetti, typically served as a main course, weighing approximately 283 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with marinara sauce and seafood (FDC ID 172922)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry FDC ID 172922 'Pasta with marinara sauce and seafood', as 'Scorching Ocean Spaghetti' is a specific fusion dish not available in primary Malaysian or global food databases. This provides a reasonable approximation for a mixed seafood pasta dish. Values are adapted to reflect the spicy Malaysian-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — pasta and mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated at 50, based on values for similar mixed pasta dishes (e.g., pasta with meat sauce GI 48; spaghetti boiled al dente GI 46) published in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr). The protein and fat contribution from mixed seafood are expected to moderate the overall glycemic response relative to plain pasta.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion sizes of this dish due to its sodium content (~279 mg per 100 g) and consider requesting lower-sodium preparations or reducing sauce volume.

type2_diabetes.guidance: For Type 2 Diabetes management, portion control is important given the moderate carbohydrate load (~12.8 g per 100 g, GI ~50); pairing with non-starchy vegetables and lean seafood protein can help attenuate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Those with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; the saturated fat (~0.61 g per 100 g) and sodium (~279 mg per 100 g) warrant attention, and choosing preparations with olive oil and lean seafood can improve the lipid profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Scorching Ocean Spaghetti is a modern fusion dish served at contemporary Malaysian cafes and restaurants, blending Italian-style spaghetti with bold, spicy Malaysian-inspired seafood flavours. The dish typically features al dente spaghetti tossed in a rich chili-tomato or sambal-based sauce, loaded with mixed seafood such as prawns, squid, and fish fillets. The 'scorching' descriptor highlights its piquant heat level, appealing to diners who enjoy spicy cuisine. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~104 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from the seafood (~6.8 g per 100 g), and carbohydrates from the pasta (~12.8 g per 100 g) with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 50. Sodium content is notable at ~279 mg per 100 g, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The seafood component contributes vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc. This dish is suitable as a lunch or dinner main course and is commonly found in Singaporean and Malaysian cafe menus.
common_user_queries: Nutritional value of spicy seafood pasta

confidence_reason: Nutrient and GI data are estimated from a closely analogous generic 'Pasta with marinara sauce and seafood' (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172922) and similar pasta dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database, as 'Scorching Ocean Spaghetti' is a specific proprietary fusion dish not found in primary Malaysian or global food composition databases. Multilingual names are conceptual translations of 'Spicy Seafood Spaghetti' into the required Singaporean languages.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry (FDC ID 172922). Polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry (FDC ID 172922). Trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry (FDC ID 172922). Is_halal, is_kosher, and is_dairy_free are set to false as specific information about the preparation and ingredients (e.g., type of oil, sauce base) is unknown for this proprietary dish. The dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine based on the input source (field survey data, EC Coffee Bar) and its fusion character; multilingual names follow Singaporean mandatory language rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).

Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
